text:
I suspect this is the sort of project where Loyc tools would be useful, because there's nothing really Python-specific about the goal here, and since it's Python, you can't (in general) be sure what the types are - so I guess that if you used a <http://loyc.net/loyc-trees/|Loyc tree> as the input to your prediction black box, it might work just as well on other languages.

But OTOH maybe you've just got a big AI model trained directly on source text and it doesn't actually know or care about the tree structure of the code?

Mind you an input module for Python doesn't actually exist, I'm just saying that _in principle_ it looks like a good fit... what do you think?

text: I don't have sound in the video. What's the concept here?

elements.text: I don't have sound in the video. What's the concept here?

text:
Thanks for the feedback Konrad!

I would say at this stage of the game the 2-D/3-D aspect is
important for the UI of programming. It's like WYSIWYG of
programming.

In Tree, scope has a semantic meaning but also a geometric
meaning—if things are down and to the right, they belong
together.

In Tree, there aren't infinite ways to write a list. There's
just one, and it's the way you would do it in the real
world: put items next to each other.

The languages follow constraints imposed by geometry. While
this may seem like it would limit creativity, in fact I've
found the opposite. By letting you focus on signal, and
removing all noise, you can focus on the semantics, and
really simplify your code to its essence.

There are also lots more you can do when you break out of
1-dimensional, linearly parsed languages. Tree Notation is a
subset of Grid Notation. There are other 2-D notations
out there.

An example might help. Would be easier to do a visual
but could try to put it into words. Imagine a project
wouldn't be a folder with a bunch of files, but instead one
file that you would open with a spreadsheet like IDE. and
edit like a canvas. What you'd normally put into files you
would instead drop an "anchor" in some cell in the sheet,
and start typing. That would be a root tree. And you could
have many of them throughout the canvas, instead of 1 file.
This is a good structure for languages for simulation. There
are some things like this, but none of them (AFAIK) have
ever made the layout and code isomorphic, and when you do
that you don't give up anything, and you make parsing and
tool building a lot easier.

text:
Now there are also implications for hardware, way down the
line, but at this stage it's about making things that ditch
a lot of complexity without sacrificing power, and present
the user with a code and interface that is a lot more
isomorphic to our brains which process info in a 2-D and 3-D
way.

text:
> *Perhaps all programming is distributed programming?*
Yes, yes, yes!

Its just that we have established models and tools to create a "single computer" abstraction out of _certain kinds_ of smaller computers, e.g. the "PC" bundle.

One way I look at this is to see a bundle as having observable, consistent states and "inner bundles". Consider this statement in a C like language:
`i = j + 1`
This "C-system" (language + runtime) executes the above statement - you have values for i and j, then after the step, you have a new value for i. To simplify you have the "state before" and "state after". But if we look at the same system as a collection of "inner-systems", the transition consists of various nested states such as moving bits from memory to a register, an operation on the register, then moving it back to anther place in memory. Now the question is: if the first MOV has happened, but addition has not, what is the state _in terms of the C-computer?_ It's not inconsistent, maybe it's in transition? Certainly it is on its way to being consistent and visible in outer system. We can go deeper than MOV - a register isn't a single physical array of memory in the CPU (neither is a "location in memory") - theres caches and other kinds of _inner-systems_ all the way. One observable transition in the outer system contains many transitions in the inner systems.

> What does the UX for programming look like if you also have to specify where computations happen?
This has been on my mind too and I think for certain parts of programs, we specifically don't want to specify where the computation happens. This is what I sometimes call _topology_ _independent_ programs. Note that location presumes a addressing space. Above, in the C-system, there is no location (the C-system is whole world) and when we zoom into it, we see locations such as cpu, ram, AX, BX or whatever. The compiler made that mapping for us - it organized the inner systems to appear as if its one outer system. (But we could have too, and there are many alternative locations and ways to map that would have all worked out just fine). However if we zoom out from the C-system to a distributed database (or whatever it is part of), the single C-system is now one location in a larger address space, but the database-system will have location independent concepts like a _table_. I think to specify _where_ computation happens, we need to build this idea of mapping the outer-system meaning to the inner-system meaning and distinct address spaces. To do this in a general way would mean we can always scale out to the next level using the same mapping ideas as we did at the previous step out.

text: this is a great thread and i’m mostly commenting to get notified, but the one thing i’d like to point out is that for whatever reason, it’s pretty _easy_ to forget (or never learn) that all these chips are working together. it’s pretty rare that just the microcontroller for your ram stick needs to be restarted, and it’s even rarer that a failing stick corrupts your word documents. are there any system design lessons we can learn from that? why is it easier to make these smaller and cheaper components reliable than it is to make servers reliable?

text:
The single-process serial computation (aka "Turing machine") is a useful abstraction. It corresponds nicely to how people perform symbolic manipulations by hand. It's also much easier to reason about than distributed computing models.

Given that the Turing Machine abstraction won't (and shouldn't) go away, let me rephrase the topic of this thread: should it be the dominant abstraction for developing software system? Certainly not. Perhaps the mistake is to consider it a more fundamental abstraction than distributed models, and try to implement the latter in terms of the former. Perhaps it should be the other way round: implement the Turing Machine as a high-level abstraction on top of a more powerful one, as a useful simplification to use when appropriate.

text: You are spot on. Our tool is for Python at the moment. But in principle, it can be extended easily to other Languages. I did not know of about Loyc tree. Thanks for the pointers. I had studied two system trying to build the similar functions. One is the (now-defunct) Babelfish (I could not find anymore the original website, but I found this one - <https://dpordomingo.gitbook.io/community-edition/usage/bblfsh>) and tree-sitter (<https://tree-sitter.github.io/tree-sitter/>) docly internally depends on tree-sitter via the open source library tree-hugger (<https://github.com/autosoft-dev/tree-hugger>). The last one was also created and open sourced by us (Codist)

attachments.title: autosoft-dev/tree-hugger
attachments.text: A light-weight, extendable, high level, universal code parser built on top of tree-sitter - autosoft-dev/tree-hugger

text: Interesting! Thanks. Something I'm focusing more on in 2021 is to get the conversation started about #abolishcopyright and #abolishpatents. I don't want to have that discussion here, since it can get pretty political, however I have found that there are a lot of people who have come to the conclusions that I have, that reform isn't the way to go and we need to fully abolish. Given that there are no organizations yet (at least in the US, AFAIK—I think worldwide the Pirate Party may be more established), working on that and giving a voice to people who have that stance, if you happen to already agree feel free to get in touch (<mailto:breck7@gmail.com|breck7@gmail.com>). Again, if you don't have that stance would love to debate it, but not here. Think politics should stay out of FOC :slightly_smiling_face:
